verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 10-14 show multiple close-up photos of curved terracotta Mangalore tiles (dark red, semi-cylindrical profile). Page 7 and 8 also show the headroom structure with the same Mangalore tile roof.
verdicts.rationale: Mangalore tiled roof is unambiguously confirmed across at least six photos; curved terracotta tiles are clearly visible in pages 10-14.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_answer: details: The obstruction on the roof is sloped roof that could affect the solar installation
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 10-14 show the sloped Mangalore tile surface with no water tanks, AC units, or large overhead obstructions on the proposed panel areas. The slope itself is the declared obstruction. The headroom parapet wall is visible adjacent to the panel area (pages 10, 13) but is incorporated into the layout.
verdicts.rationale: Designer identifies the slope as the obstruction; photos confirm sloped Mangalore tile roof with no additional major obstructions visible on the proposed installation area.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing roof is 23 degree slope
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 11-14 show side-on views of the sloped Mangalore tile roof. The visual tilt appears moderate - roughly 20-25 degrees, consistent with a typical Mangalore tile roof pitch. The 3D side view on page 19 also depicts a comparable slope angle.
verdicts.rationale: Visual tilt of the tile slope in pages 11-14 is consistent with a ~23 degree pitch; no photographic contradiction found.

verdicts.question: In case of sloped roof, what is the slope angle of the roof? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Same photo evidence as Q3 - sloped Mangalore tile roof visible in pages 10-14 with pitch visually consistent with ~23 degrees.
verdicts.rationale: Slope angle of approximately 23 degrees is consistent with the visual pitch observed in multiple roof close-up photos.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing two roof are located in same building
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 top-view diagram shows two distinct tiled roof sections with panels placed on both. Pages 19 (side view and ISO view) 3D renders also clearly show solar panels on two separate tiled roof sections of the same building.
verdicts.rationale: Top-view layout diagram (page 18) and 3D renders (page 19) unambiguously show two separate tiled roof sections with solar panels on the same building.

verdicts.question: If it is a Tiled roof, what is underneath the tiles?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 10 and 13 show RCC concrete parapet walls immediately adjacent to the tiled roof sections. The overall construction quality visible in exterior photos (pages 5, 21) is consistent with a well-constructed RCC-framed villa. However, the sub-tile structure is not directly visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: RCC parapet walls visible are consistent with an RCC sub-structure, but the material directly beneath the tiles cannot be confirmed from photos alone.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 10-14 show bare Mangalore tile roof surfaces with no existing mounting hardware. The proposed area is unmarked bare tile. No photo shows mini-rail hardware installed or staged.
verdicts.rationale: Mounting structure is a planned item; photos show only bare tiles in the proposed area. Mini-rail suitability for Mangalore tiles is standard practice but cannot be photo-verified from this document.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.designer_notes: North side four panels and south side four panels
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 top-view diagram includes a north compass arrow. The two tiled roof sections with panels are arranged facing opposite directions - one toward the south and one toward the north relative to the compass shown. Pages 19 (ISO and side view renders) confirm panels on both sides of the ridge.
verdicts.rationale: Top-view diagram with north compass (page 18) and 3D renders (page 19) confirm panels placed on opposing roof faces, consistent with South and North orientation.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 shows the interior marble staircase with glass railing with caption 'This route (using staircase) will be followed to carry small items.' Page 9 shows the staircase with annotated width of 0.86M.
verdicts.rationale: Interior staircase clearly visible in pages 7 and 9 with explicit caption confirming it is the access route.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: All photos show a fully completed and occupied villa - painted exterior walls (pages 5, 21), finished tiled roof, furnished interior visible through windows (page 8), a car parked in the driveway. No bare brick, scaffolding, or construction materials visible anywhere.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple photos confirm a fully constructed, finished, and occupied residential villa with no signs of construction activity.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 (front exterior) and page 21 (building exterior) show the building facade: ground floor with carport, first floor with windows, second floor visible, and the headroom/penthouse structure with tiled roof visible at the top. Page 6 (looking up from ground) also confirms the multi-storey height. Total of 3 above-ground floors plus a headroom room is consistent with G+2+Headroom.
verdicts.rationale: Exterior photos (pages 5, 6, 21) show ground floor + 2 upper floors + a headroom structure with tiled roof, consistent with G+2+Headroom description. 12m height is plausible for this configuration.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photos (pages 5 and 21) show a residential villa in a gated community with Mangalore tiled roof, G+2+Headroom structure, and a carport - all consistent with the building description of a residential project at Nambiar Bellezea.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photos clearly match the described residential villa with Mangalore tiled roof and multi-floor structure.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Exterior photos on pages 5, 15, and 21 show the area around the building. No overhead high-tension power lines are visible in any photo. The site appears to be in a modern gated community (Nambiar Bellezea) with underground utilities.
verdicts.rationale: No overhead HT lines visible in any exterior or surrounding-area photo. Modern gated community layout is consistent with underground utilities.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 (material delivery route photo) shows a wide paved road in front of the villa with a car already parked. The caption confirms this route is for material delivery. Page 21 building exterior also shows the same wide road frontage.
verdicts.rationale: Page 5 delivery route photo shows a wide paved road directly in front of the villa, clearly accessible to delivery vehicles.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 shows the side passage is narrow with some vegetation but the primary lifting route is via rope/crane at the front (page 5 caption: 'using lifting'). The staircase width is 0.86M (page 9), which is narrow but workable for small items. No major fixed obstacles (low-hanging structures, locked gates, machinery) visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: No significant lifting obstacles identified in photos; the narrow side passage is noted but the main panel delivery route uses rope lifting from the front road.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Yes, the location of the earth pits in garden area which is north facing
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 Earth Pit Location photo shows a narrow side garden alley between buildings with bare soil and potted plants. 'DC Earth Pit' and 'LA Pit' are clearly labeled on the photo. The top-view diagram on page 18 with north compass shows this area is on the north/side of the building.
verdicts.rationale: Page 18 earth pit photo shows soil/garden area with DC Earth Pit and LA Pit locations marked; top-view compass confirms this is on the north side of the building, consistent with designer's answer.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo of internet router visible in the document. This is marked as Customer Scope.
verdicts.rationale: No photo evidence of router location; Customer Scope items are not typically documented with photos in the TSVR.

verdicts.question: What's the Wi-Fi strength of the router near to the String inverter/Envoy?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo showing Wi-Fi signal strength measurement visible in the document.
verdicts.rationale: Wi-Fi signal strength cannot be visually verified from site photos; no signal measurement photo provided.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo of WhatsApp location pin visible in the document.
verdicts.rationale: WhatsApp location pin cannot be verified from site photos; this is a process step not captured photographically in the TSVR.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: Normal Three Phase
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 9 Meter Box photo shows a dual-compartment panel cabinet. The left compartment contains a meter unit with a green LCD/LED digital display (appears to be a Crabtree brand digital meter). The right compartment shows multiple MCBs and wiring consistent with a 3-phase distribution board. The panel complexity and number of MCBs is consistent with a 3-phase setup.
verdicts.rationale: Page 9 meter box photo shows a digital meter (green LCD display visible) in a multi-MCB panel consistent with a 3-phase digital meter installation.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 9 meter box photo shows a panel consistent with a 3-phase digital meter setup - digital display meter on left, 3-phase MCB distribution on right. This matches the described Three Phase Digital meter.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photo (page 9) is consistent with a 3-phase digital meter arrangement, supporting the designer's self-confirmation.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 Equipment Location photo clearly labels 'Main Meter' (red overlay) and 'Solar Meter' (cyan overlay) positions side-by-side in the wall-mounted cubicle, indicating the termination point has been identified and the solar meter placement relative to the main meter is planned.
verdicts.rationale: Page 17 equipment location photo explicitly labels Main Meter and Solar Meter positions, confirming the termination point has been identified.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 Equipment Location shows an open wall-mounted shelf/cubicle with the meter panel accessible and visible. The area is not behind a sealed wall or locked enclosure in the photo.
verdicts.rationale: Page 17 shows the meter panel area as an accessible, open cubicle, consistent with being 'Visible' rather than sealed.

verdicts.question: In case of an Enphase system, is the distance between the farthest micro-inverter and IQ Gateway > 50m?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 top-view layout shows the overall footprint of the building. The building is a compact residential villa and the total building span is clearly well under 50m. The farthest panel placement is visible in the 3D renders (pages 18-19) and is within the villa's compact footprint.
verdicts.rationale: Site layout and building footprint (page 18) confirm a compact residential villa where the micro-inverter to gateway distance would be well under 50m.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 Equipment Location photo explicitly marks 'Solar Meter' (cyan box) as a distinct space next to the 'Main Meter' (red box) in the wall-mounted panel area. A separate shelving position is visibly available.
verdicts.rationale: Page 17 explicitly shows a designated Solar Meter space adjacent to the Main Meter, confirming space availability.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available to house the Enphase accessories or ACDB components?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 equipment location photo shows the panel area has additional shelving/cabinet space beyond the Main Meter and identified Solar Meter slot. The overall cabinet appears to have available space.
verdicts.rationale: Page 17 panel area shows multiple cabinet slots with the Main Meter and Solar Meter identified, and the surrounding space appears adequate for additional Enphase/ACDB components.

verdicts.question: Is there space available to dig DC, AC and LA earth pits?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 Earth Pit Location photo shows a garden/soil strip along the side of the building with 'DC Earth Pit' and 'LA Pit' labeled. The bare soil area with plants is clearly present and appears sufficient for earth pit excavation.
verdicts.rationale: Page 18 earth pit photo explicitly shows the soil garden area with DC Earth Pit and LA Pit locations marked, confirming space availability.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 shows a well-maintained interior marble staircase with glass railing. Page 6 shows the terrace level has concrete parapet walls. Pages 8 and 10 show the rooftop terrace area with parapet walls. The sloped tiled roof sections (pages 10-14) do not show any additional safety hazards. No exposed electrical wires, fragile structures, or unusual access hazards are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase access appears safe (marble stairs, glass rail, page 7); terrace has parapet walls (pages 6, 8); no unusual safety hazards visible in any photo.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 10 'Material Storage' section shows the rooftop terrace area with open flat tiled space in the foreground - sufficient for laying panels horizontally. The terrace level (visible in pages 6, 8) provides flat open space consistent with horizontal panel staging.
verdicts.rationale: Page 10 Material Storage photo shows open flat rooftop terrace space, consistent with horizontal panel staging ('horizontally sleeping').

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 shows the equipment location with Main Meter and Solar Meter labeled. The wall area appears compact and consistent with an 850mm x 950mm footprint, but no measurement annotations are visible in the photo to confirm exact dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Solar meter cubicle location is photographically shown (page 17) but exact dimensions of 850x950mm cannot be verified without measurement annotations in the photo.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 18 Earth Pit Location photo shows the garden strip between buildings. The strip appears to have sufficient length for earth pit excavation, but the exact available space dimensions (1500x1500mm) cannot be confirmed without measurement annotations.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location is photographically confirmed (page 18) but the exact 1500x1500mm dimensions of the available space cannot be verified from the photo alone.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing building shadow will fall on the panels during second half of every month
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 20 shows dedicated Shadow Analysis diagrams (top-down and ISO views). The 3D shadow models clearly show the headroom/penthouse structure casting a shadow on the adjacent tiled roof panel sections. The site observation notes (page 21) confirm 'the customer has confirmed that shading on the panels is acceptable.'
verdicts.rationale: Dedicated Shadow Analysis section (page 20) with 3D models explicitly shows building shadow falling on panel areas, and page 21 customer acceptance note confirms the shadow scenario is acknowledged.
